sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Post-injury hand screening should identify who is suitable for conservative mobilisation and swelling management and who may have tendon, fracture, or nerve problems requiring specialist hand review.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Stiffness, swelling, pain, or weakness after hand or finger injury
- Reduced grip, pinch, or dexterity
- Scar sensitivity, tendon glide restriction, or guarded use
- Difficulty returning to tools, keyboards, or fine motor tasks

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Escalate for tendon rupture, fracture instability, infection, major sensory loss, or complex regional pain features
- Hand stiffness can become a major functional problem even after seemingly small injuries
- Overprotection can worsen long-term hand function

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Screen skin, swelling, neurovascular status, and whether the hand is safe to use lightly today
- Encourage protected use within limits rather than total avoidance when safe
- Escalate if there is loss of active tendon function, severe pain, or major deformity

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Assess tendon excursion, edema, range, scar sensitivity, and task-specific function
- Use graded motion, edema control, desensitisation, and strengthening matched to the stage of healing
- Refer for hand surgery or specialist therapy review when tendon, fracture, nerve, or complex pain concerns persist

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - Hand recovery should be judged by real pinch, grip, and dexterity demands, not swelling alone
